coredata.dc:title: Folic acid conjugated carbon dots doped metal-organic framework materials for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, J.
Cheng, Q.
Zhu, C.C.
Huang, H.Y.
Su, J.B.
Ni, W.
Ruan, B.
Ma, N.
Tsai, F.C.
coredata.dc:description: Folate receptor is one of the common biomarkers, which can be used for early diagnosis of cancer. Folic acid has bind specifically to folate receptor. Herein, folic acid is co-conjugated with carbon dots (FA-CDs) to achieve fluorescence imaging of tumor cells because CDs have excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. In consideration of complex environment of organisms, metal-organic framework material as the host material is used for improving the stability of FA-CDs. In view of the negatively charged FA-CDs, positively charged UiO-66-NH2 was chosen as host material. Fluorescent CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea using microwave method. And folic acid was conjugated with CDs by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide. FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material was obtained by adsorption method, which had a great stability and anti-interference capability under different salt concentrations, pH, and coexistence substances. And FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 had low toxicity in both normal cells and tumor cells. Concurrently, composite material could realize fluorescence imaging in RAW cells and COS-7 cells, manifesting FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 possessed the capable of fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: UiO-66-NH2
Carbon dots
Folic acid
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study has been supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( SQ2020YFF0413357 ); Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Economic Forest Germplasm Improvement and Comprehensive Utilization of Resources , Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for the Characteristic Resources Exploitation of Dabie Mountains, Huanggang Normal University , Huanggang 438000, China ( 202020504 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The UV-Vis of CDs, FA, and FA-CDs; (b) the FT-IR of CDs, FA, and FA-CDs. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid; FT-IR, Fourier transform infrared.
(a) The zeta potential of CDs, FA, and FA-CDs; (b) The TGA of CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 .CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid; TGA, thermogravimetric analysis.

The FE-SEM pictures of UiO-66-NH2 (a) and FA-CDs@ UiO-66-NH2 (b); The TEM pictures of UiO-66-NH2 (c) and FA-CDs@ UiO-66-NH2 (d). CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid; FE-SEM, field emission scanning electron microscope; TEM, transmission electron microscope.
(a) The PXRD of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (b) the FT-IR of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid; PXRD, power X-ray diffraction.
The zeta potentials of UiO-66-NH2, FA-CDs, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
(a), (b) The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (c), (d) the pore size distribution of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs in PBS and H2O. CD, carbon dot.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of UiO-66-NH2 (a) and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (b) under different excitation wavelengths. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg/mL) under different concentrations of NaCl (0 M represented pH = 7.4 PBS solution). CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg/mL) at different pH solutions. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
The fluorescence emission spectrum of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 (1 mg/mL) under different coexistence substances. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.

(a), (b) Cell viability of COS-7 cells in different concentrations of CDs, FA-CDs, UIO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (c), (d) cell viability of RAW cells in different concentrations of CDs, FA-CDs, UIO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.

(a) Bright field diagram of RAW cells incubated with CDs, FA-CDs, UIO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (b) Fluorescence imaging of RAW cells with CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at 405 nm excitation wavelength; (c) Bright field diagram of COS-7 cells incubated with CDs, FA-CDs, UIO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2; (d) Fluorescence imaging of COS-7 cells with CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 at 405 nm excitation wavelength. (Scale Bar: 20 μm). CD, carbon dot; FA, folic acid.
The synthetic schematic diagram of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2

head.ce:title: Folic acid conjugated carbon dots doped metal-organic framework materials for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials (Ministry of Education), Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed By the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed By the Province and Ministry
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials (Ministry of Education), Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed By the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Laboratory, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folate receptor is one of the common biomarkers, which can be used for early diagnosis of cancer. Folic acid has bind specifically to folate receptor. Herein, folic acid is co-conjugated with carbon dots (FA-CDs) to achieve fluorescence imaging of tumor cells because CDs have excellent fluorescence properties, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility. In consideration of complex environment of organisms, metal-organic framework material as the host material is used for improving the stability of FA-CDs. In view of the negatively charged FA-CDs, positively charged UiO-66-NH2 was chosen as host material. Fluorescent CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea using microwave method. And folic acid was conjugated with CDs by 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride and N-hydroxysuccinimide. FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material was obtained by adsorption method, which had a great stability and anti-interference capability under different salt concentrations, pH, and coexistence substances. And FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 had low toxicity in both normal cells and tumor cells. Concurrently, composite material could realize fluorescence imaging in RAW cells and COS-7 cells, manifesting FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 possessed the capable of fluorescence imaging.

The synthetic schematic diagram of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 and (a) Bright field diagram of RAW cells incubated and (b) Fluorescence imaging of RAW cells at 405 nm excitation wavelength. (Scale Bar: 20 μm).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a hot topic and an important public health problem worldwide [1]. Nowadays, the success rate of cancer treatment in the early phase is relatively high, so the early diagnosis of cancer has become the crucial method to find and receive treatment for cancer [2]. Hence, it is very important to detect tumor cells in the early stages of cancer. Folic acid (FA) receptor as a common tumor target marker also provides a favorable basis for early diagnosis of cancer [3], which is mainly due to the overexpression of folate receptors on tumor cells compared to normal cells [4]. Because FA is a water-soluble metabolically stable vitamin and its molecular weight is relatively small [5,6]. Furthermore, FA have a specific binding with folate receptor and can easily enter cells by endocytosis of receptors [7,8]. These advantages lay a theoretical foundation for the realization of fluorescence imaging of tumor cells. In addition, fluorescence imaging can be achieved by coupling the fluorescent material with FA.

At present, there are many kinds of fluorescent materials developed, among which carbon dots (CDs) are more popular. In previous reports, conventional fluorescent dyes have been applied to cell imaging experiments [9,10]. However, with the development of quantum dots, they gradually fade out of the scope of research. But semiconductor quantum dots are relatively high cytotoxic even at low concentrations because they contain heavy metals such as arsenic and cadmium [11,12]. Compared with traditional fluorescent materials, the appearance of carbon quantum dots became a research hotpot. CDs have the advantages of low toxicity, superior fluorescence performance, and good biocompatibility [13–17]. FA is a highly electronegative substance with both amino and carboxyl functional groups and multiple functional groups can coexist on the surface of fluorescent CDs [18]. In consequence, FA can be conjugated with CDs to attain the fluorescence imaging of tumor cells [3]. However, the internal environment of organisms is complex. In order to give better play to the properties of FA-CDs, it is urgent to find a suitable host material to enhance the stability, biocompatibility, and anti-interference ability of FA-CDs.

Porous materials, especially metal-organic framework (MOF) materials have attracted more attention [19,20]. MOF materials are self-assembled by metal ions and organic ligands [21–23]. The types of metal ions and organic ligands are diverse in this process [24]. MOFs have rich topological structures by changing the composition of metal ions or organic ligands, which also expands the application field of MOFs materials [25]. In addition, functionalization of organic ligands can also improve the properties of MOFs [26,27]. Meanwhile, functionalized MOFs materials can provide more binding sites as host materials so that they can better enrich the guest materials to enhance their sensitivity and stability. MOFs are mostly applied in chemical sensing, gas adsorption, battery, and other fields [28–32]. At the same time, MOFs have been widely researched in biological field due to their good biodegradability, biocompatibility, luminescence, and functionalization [33–35]. Moreover, the adjustable pore size and high porosity of MOFs can also be used in drug loading and delivery applications [36–39].

In recent years, MOFs as the main material to prepare composite materials is a hot hand research in cell imaging, drug loading, and other fields. Dong et al. [40] reported the synthesis of FA-NMOFs to construct active tumor-targeted NMOFs drug carriers. MOF-808 and UiO-66-NH2 were selected as the main materials, and FA molecules were anchored on the surface of MOFs by coordinating the terminal carboxylate in FA molecules with Zr6 cluster. Concurrently, targeted delivery of anticancer drugs to tumor cells could be achieved by loading 5-FU into FA-NMOFs. Li et al. [41] developed novel NMOFs for the detection of cysteine and glutathione in aqueous solutions. And NMOFs has fluorescence imaging ability for cysteine and glutathione in living cells.

Herein, CDs was synthesized from citric acid and urea using microwave method. 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were using as coupling agents, FA-CDs with negative charge was successfully synthesized by coupling CDs with FA. Previous studies in the biological field with UiO-66 as the main material have been reported, indicating that UiO-66 has potential in biological aspects [42,43]. Considering the surface potential of FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2 as a positively charged MOFs was selected to synthesize FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material. At the same time, the amination of UiO 66 has good luminescence properties and can provide more binding sites after amination, so as to enrich FA-CDs and improve the sensitivity of the material. The fluorescence intensity of composite material was measured under different salt concentration, different pH and coexisting substance. It was found that the composite had good stability and anti-interference ability. In addition, the cytotoxicity test proved that the material had low toxicity and good cytocompatibility, and the biological imaging experiment showed that the FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material had good cell imaging ability on RAW cells and COS-7 cells.

Zirconium chloride (ZrCl4) was purchased from Aladdin. 2-Amino terephthalic acid was bought from Macklin. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) was purchased from Accela ChemBio Co., Ltd. EDC HCl was purchased from Shanghai Dibai Biological Technology Co., LTD. FA, urea, citric acid, acetic acid, hydrochloric acid, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and absolute ethanol (EtOH) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents were analytical grade. Deionized water was laboratory-made. DMEM medium, trypsin and fetal bovine serum were purchased from Invitrogen company.

The power X-ray diffraction (PXRD) was obtained using Bruker D8A25 (air atmosphere) X-ray powder diffractometer made in Germany. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured by Mettler Toledo TGA1. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was acquired by Nicolet-iS 50. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrum was measured using TU-1810 ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The zeta potential was obtained by nanoparticle size and potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90). The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller-specific surface area was gained by QDS-MP-30. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired by F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer. (3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was obtained by Model 550 in Bio-Rad (USA). The cell imaging was measured by Nikon C1-si (Japan). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) pictures were gained by G20S-TWIN (America). The quantum yield of CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were obtained using the stable/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Fluo Time300).

CDs were prepared via microwave method. 0.5 g citric acid and 0.3 g urea were ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL water. Then the solution was microwaved for 5 min. After cooling, adding 20 mL water and the solution was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The solution was dialyzed with 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. Then the pure CDs solution was obtained, which was froze drying for follow-up experiment.

The synthesis of FA-CDs was referred by previous literature [44]. 24 mg of FA, 12 mg of CDs, 9 mg of EDC•HCl and 9 mg of NHS were added into 30 mL PBS (Phosphate Buffered Saline, 0.01 M pH = 7.4) and stirred for 24 h. Then the FA-CDs solution was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove unreacted FA. The solution was dialyzed with 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h (the temperature of the overall process was maintained at 25 °C). And the FA-CDs solution was froze drying for the next experiment.

UiO-66-NH2 was fabricated by using microwave method. 0.54 mmol ZrCl4 was scattered in the 5 mL DMF and 1 mL HCl (denoted as A), 0.75 mmol 2-amino terephthalic acid was dispersed in the 10 mL DMF (Expressed as B). And A and B were mixed ultrasonically. Then the mixed solution was reacted at 100 °C for 2 h in the MCR-3 atmospheric microwave chemical reactor. After the reaction was ended, the resulting sample was washed three times by DMF and absolute ethyl alcohol separately. The powder sample was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h.

The synthesis of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was prepared by utilizing water bath adsorption method. 3 mg FA-CDs was prepared 100 mL solution in the volumetric flask. 100 mg UiO-66-NH2 was added to the 100 mL FA-CDs solution, which was put in the water bath kettle at 37 °C for 24 h. Then the sample was washed several times to remove unabsorbed FA-CDs. The powder FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was dried under vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h.

The MTT assay was conducted in COS-7 cells and RAW cells to perform cytotoxicity experiments. COS-7 is a normal cell and its cell line was derived from African green monkey kidney fibroblasts [45]. Its growth characteristics were adherent cells and cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. RAW, namely mouse macrophages, is tumor cell. They were grown characteristics as adherent cells and the culture medium was the same as that of COS-7 cells [46]. COS-7 cells and RAW cells were implanted in 96-well plates at a cell density of 6 × 103 per well separately and incubated in 100 μL of DMEM medium. After 24 h, the medicine materials (CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2) were added into the well of two cells, respectively. After co-incubation for 24 h, 20 μL MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS solution) was added into each well of two cells and incubated for 4 h. And then the medium was removed and 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide was added; the absorbance was determined at 570 nm by microplate reader and calculated the cell viability. The cell viability was calculated as: (sample group OD570/control group OD570) × 100%. In the control group, cells without agent treatment were directly treated with PBS.

RAW cells were added into 6-well plates at a cell density of 5 × 105 per well and co-cultured with 2 mL DMEM medium. After incubation for 24 h, the agent (CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2) were added in the well at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. After co-incubation for 6 h, the medicine materials were discarded and washed the cells with fresh medium and PBS three times separately. The confocal microscope images of the four medicine materials in the emission band of 420–500 nm were collected using 405 nm UV light as the excitation wavelength.

The synthesized FA-CDs are characterized to judge whether the coupling of FA and CDs was successful. The coupling as-synthesized FA-CDs is confirmed by UV–Vis, FT-IR, Zeta potential, and TGA. As shown in Fig. 1 , the TEM image of CDs is tested. It can be seen that CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed. The UV–Vis of CDs, FA, and FA-CDs is explained that FA-CDs have the characteristic absorption peak of CDs at 340 nm. From the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 b), the C=N characteristic peak of FA-CDs is corresponded to the poison structure of FA at about 1680 cm−1. But CDs is not possessed C=N absorption peak. Then the zeta potential and TGA data are obtained as shown in Fig. 3 . FA is a highly electronegative substance. Through the zeta potential, the surface potentials of FA and CDs are negative charge. Evidently, the surface potential of FA-CDs is declined compared to CDs, which may be due to the –NH2 of CDs is combined with electronegative FA [1]. As displayed in Fig. 3b, TGA is measured under O2 atmosphere. The curve of FA-CDs has weight loss at about 250 °C due to the decomposition of FA [40]. The weight percentage of FA conjugated to CDs is 13.57 wt% approximately. In consequence, the coupling of FA and CDs is successful from the result of all above test data. Synchronously, the successful synthesis of FA-CDs has provided a basis for preparing FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material.

Schematic diagram of the synthesis of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 was displayed in Scheme 1 . FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 has been constructed by the self-assembly of FA-CDs and UiO-66-NH2. Due to the specific binding between FA and FA receptor, the composite material can be used for fluorescence imaging of biological tumor cells in vitro. Initially, the morphology images of the samples were tested as shown in Fig. 4 . The structure of UiO-66-NH2 is tended to be spherical due to the amino group functionalizes. In addition, the effect of FA-CDs coupling on UiO-66-NH2 morphology was investigated by TEM test. It is obvious that the adsorption of FA-CDs does not affect the original morphology of UiO-66-NH2. And because of the high contrast of MOFs material, CDs do not develop on it [47–49]. In consequence, FT-IR, Zeta potential, TGA, and other tests are carried out to prove that FA-CDs and UiO-66-NH2 are successfully combined. PXRD is implemented to confirm FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 has been successfully synthesized. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the PXRD diffraction peak of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material match well with the simulated UiO-66-NH2 demonstrating the successful synthesis of sample. Furthermore, FI-IR testing has been carried out as exhibited in Fig. 5b. FA-CDs possesses the special bond C=N. Obviously, it can be seen that FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 has the particular C=N bond of FA-CDs from FT-IR spectra, which is further proved that the sample is successfully synthesized. TGA measurements have been implemented to test the thermostability of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 under O2 atmosphere. As displayed in Fig. 3b, UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 can keep the crystal structure before 500 °C, indicating the two materials have great thermal stability. From the result of TGA, the weight percentage of FA-CDs attached to UiO-66-NH2 is about 2.13 wt%. In order to further verify UiO-66-NH2 is successfully encapsulated with FA-CDs, the zeta potential has been measured. Fig. 6 is shown the surface potential of UiO-66-NH2 is positive charge and FA-CDs is negative charge. Theoretically, if the FA-CDs are encapsulated in the hole of UiO-66-NH2, indicating the surface potential of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material becomes more positive. But the surface negative potential of composite material becomes larger than FA-CDs, which is declared that FA-CDs is encapsulated outside the hole of UiO-66-NH2. Simultaneously, the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms are executed to test the porosity and the pore size of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. The specific surface areas of samples have been calculated from N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K. As shown in Fig. 7 a and b, the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller specific surface area of UiO-66-NH2 is 724.983 m2/g. After doping FA-CDs, the specific surface area of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 is 686.074 m2/g, where the specific surface area is decreased significantly compared with UiO-66-NH2. The pore size distribution is exhibited in Fig. 7c and d. The dV/dW value of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 is far less than UiO-66-NH2 when the pore width is about 0.8 nm, which may be due to the fact that FA-CDs are dispersed outside the pore and partially block the entry of N2 during the test [50]. In addition, compared with UiO-66-NH2, the pore volume of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 is declined proving FA-CDs are encapsulated outside the hole of UiO-66-NH2, which is also exactly consistent with the conclusion of Zeta potential. It also shows that FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 is successfully prepared.

In order to judge the luminescence properties of CDs, the fluorescence properties of CDs were tested in PBS and water. As shown in Fig. 8 , CDs have the highest fluorescence intensity at about 435 nm when the excitation wavelength is 365 nm. It also shows that the CDs have blue fluorescence. In order to later conduct experiment in vitro cell imaging, all the fluorescent tests are carried out using PBS (0.01 M pH = 7.4) as solvent. The fluorescence emission spectrums have been examined under different excitation wavelengths. As displayed in Fig. 9 , UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are gained highest PL intensity when the excitation wavelength is 365 nm. At the same time, according to the emission wavelength corresponding to the highest fluorescence intensity, UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 have blue fluorescence. As a consequence, 365 nm wavelength is selected for subsequent fluorescence measurements. In addition, the quantum yields of the materials are measured and calculated by the stable/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Fluo Time300). The fluorescence quantum yields of CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are tested in PBS solution. The quantum yields of CDs and FA-CDs are 47.68% and 0.21%. Similarly, the fluorescence quantum yields of UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are 62.27% and 28.86%, respectively. For purpose of validating the stability and anti-interference capability of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2, the PL testing is done in the salt solution, pH solution, and coexisting substances. The fluorescence emission spectrums of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 are measured under different concentrations of NaCl (Fig. 10 ). Notably, the fluorescence intensity ratios IF/IF0 of other NaCl concentrations are fluctuated with 0 M NaCl as the baseline and within a small range (IF0 is the fluorescence intensity in PBS and IF are fluorescence intensity in different NaCl concentrations). Synchronously, the pH in organism is also an important factor to consider. As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence intensity ratios IF/IF0 are mainly invariable at pH change from 6 to 12, confirming the stability of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 in the pH solution (IF are fluorescence intensity in different pH solutions). In organisms, many substances are coexisted. It is crucial to consider the effect of coexisting substances on the stability of composite material. As exhibited in Fig. 12 , coexisting substances are measured including NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, glucose, glycine, and glutamic acid. Evidently, the fluorescence intensity ratios IF/IF0 are basically maintained at the same baseline compared with no coexisting substance (IF are fluorescence intensity in different coexisting substances). From the above results, the fluorescence property of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material is steady under distinct salt solution, pH solution, and coexistence substances, which also provides a favorable data basis for FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material to realize cell fluorescence imaging.

In order for the materials to be used for subsequent cell fluorescence imaging experiments, the cytotoxicity was carried on CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2. MTT assay was conducted in COS-7 cells and RAW cells. As exhibited in Fig. 13 , four materials display insignificant toxicity in COS-7 cells when the concentrations of four materials are reached 200 μg/mL. And COS-7 cells still have a survival rate of more than 90%. Despite the survival rate of tumor cells is slightly decreased compared with normal cells, the RAW cells still have more than 80% cell viability in the four materials at 200 μg/mL concentration. At the same time, it can be seen that in both tumor cells and normal cells, the cell viability of FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material is basically increased compared with that of pure FA-CDs. This indicates that the accession of UiO-66-NH2 improves the biocompatibility of FA-CDs, which is also one of the advantages of MOFs materials in the biological direction. As a result, MTT data show these four materials have low cytotoxicity in both normal cells and tumor cells, which also lay a foundation for the subsequent experimental tests of the composite materials.

The fluorescence imaging of CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 were measured using confocal laser microscope in RAW cells. Therefore, the concentrations of the four materials were 200 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. 14, (a) (c) are the bright field diagrams of the four materials in RAW and COS-7 cells. It is evidently seen that the morphology of RAW and COS-7 cells are appeared normal and intact in four materials, indicating the four materials have low toxicity. The fluorescence images of the four materials co-incubated with RAW cells are displayed in Fig. 14 (b) at 405 nm excitation wavelength. It can be discerned that the composite material with the addition of UiO-66-NH2 shows a better cell fluorescence imaging ability compared with FA-CDs, manifesting that the existence of MOFs improved the cell compatibility of the material and also confirmed the conclusion of MTT. Furthermore, the fluorescence images of COS-7 cells are displayed in Fig. 14 (d) at 405 nm excitation wavelength. FA-CDs showed almost no fluorescence in COS-7 cells. Conversely, FA-CDs showed obvious fluorescence in RAW cells. It is demonstrated FA-CDs has the targeting to tumor cells. Simultaneously, UiO-66-NH2 and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 show fluorescence imaging ability for COS-7 and RAW cells. These results indicate that FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 show good cell imaging ability in both normal cells and tumor cells.

Fluorescent CDs were synthesized using citric acid and urea as raw materials by microwave method, and FA was successfully coupled with CDs to establish FA-CDs using EDC·HCl and NHS as coupling agents. Then, UiO-66-NH2 was synthesized using zirconium chloride and 2-amino terephthalic acid. After that, FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material was synthesized utilizing adsorption method. In order for the material to have the conditions for biological applications, the fluorescence tests of the composite material were carried out under different salt concentrations, pH values and coexisting substances. Furthermore, the cytotoxicity results showed that CDs, FA-CDs, UiO-66-NH2, and FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 had low toxicity to COS-7 cells and RAW cells, while the cell imaging data showed that FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 had good imaging ability to RAW cells and COS-7 cells. In consequence, FA-CDs@UiO-66-NH2 composite material has great application prospects in the biological direction and even can be developed in biomedicine aspect in the future.

Fang-Chang Tsai and Ning Ma conceived the research, designed the experiments and co-wrote the paper. Jie Yang, Changchang Zhu and Hongyu Huang co-wrote the paper, too. Jie Yang, Wang Ni and Bo Ruan prepared and formulated the materials and carried out the PXRD, Zeta potential and surface area and pore size data and their analysis. Jie Yang and Bo Ruan performed the structural analyses and reaction mechanisms. Jie Yang and Hongyu Huang drawn the experiment flow chart. Qian Cheng, Jianbo Su and Jie Yang analyzed the results of cell viability and cell imaging. Jie Yang measured the fluorescence test and analyzed the results. All the authors discussed the results and contributed to writing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study has been supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (SQ2020YFF0413357); Hubei Provincial Key Laboratory of Economic Forest Germplasm Improvement and Comprehensive Utilization of Resources, Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for the Characteristic Resources Exploitation of Dabie Mountains, Huanggang Normal University, Huanggang 438000, China (202020504).
